notes.note: P. Servilius Vatia Isauricus, the most senior consular of the year, who had spoken first in the debate and (the speech tells us) had pushed for Gabinius and Piso to be branded by the Senate in word as well as vote.

The lex Sempronia of C. Gracchus (123 BC), which required the Senate to fix the consular provinces of the next year before the consular elections, so that the choice of provinces could not be tied to the choice of consuls. The senatorial-tribunician interposition rule that ran with it is what makes Cicero's praetorian-province alternative unworkable here.

The Roman tax-farmers, organized into companies (societates) holding the lease (redemptio) of the indirect taxes, customs, and tributes of the provinces. They were drawn largely from the equestrian order, and the consular speech assumes their interests as a major political constituency.
The legendary Assyrian queen of Babylon. The figure cuts at Gabinius's perfumed and curled effeminacy as well as his eastern campaign --- a ``Semiramis'' commanding troops in Syria.

A supplicatio: a formal day or days of thanks decreed to the immortal gods on receipt of a victory dispatch from a commander, opening their temples to the public. The Senate's refusal of the supplicatio --- here for Gabinius's letters from Syria --- was a public mark of disgrace, branding the dispatch as untrustworthy.

A province held by an ex-praetor (and so for one year) rather than by an ex-consul (typically two years or more). Cicero argues that re-grading Syria and Macedonia downward will not in fact remove Gabinius and Piso, because the rule against tribunician interposition runs only with provinces voted under the lex Sempronia.

M. Aemilius Lepidus, censor 179 BC, twice consul, pontifex maximus; the dispute and reconciliation with M. Fulvius Nobilior in the Campus Martius on the day of their election as censors was a famous exemplum of public reconciliation, recorded in Ennius's Annales (the ``highest poet'' of the section).

L. Marcius Philippus, consul 56 BC and presiding officer of this debate; the ``father'' is L. Marcius Philippus, consul 91 BC, censor 86, the orator famous for his quarrels and his reconciliations during the Social War years.

The supplicatio Cicero himself moved for Caesar's victories in Gaul: fifteen days, a length without precedent (Marius and Pompey had received ten). The motion is one of the earlier signs of Cicero's drift back into senatorial accommodation with Caesar.

C. Pomptinus, praetor 63 BC under Cicero's consulship, and (as propraetor of Transalpine Gaul) the man who broke the Allobrogian rising of 61 BC --- the conspiracy that ran into Cicero's letter-trap during the Catilinarian crisis. He had been waiting outside Rome for his triumph, which Caesar finally pushed through in November 54.

Caesar held both Cisalpine Gaul (citerior) and Transalpine Gaul (ulterior), the second added by senatorial decree on Pompey's motion after the death of Q. Metellus Celer in early 59 BC. The two rival motions sought to detach one or the other; Cicero argues both founder on the lex Sempronia framework.

The legislation of Caesar's consulship (59 BC), passed in the face of Bibulus's announcement that he was watching the heavens (a religious obstruction). Optimate jurists treated the announcement as voiding the laws, but treated the lex Clodia of 58 BC against Cicero (passed by a tribune whose adoption to the plebs had been carried by the same curiate-law procedure that depended on the auspices) as lawfully passed --- the inconsistency that Cicero presses here.

The two laws (mid-second century BC) that codified the religious-obstructionist machinery: any magistrate could observe the heavens (servare de caelo) and announce ill omens (obnuntiare) to suspend a public assembly. Clodius's tribunate of 58 BC had moved a law to gut these provisions; Cicero treats the survival of the lex Aelia and lex Fufia as one of the legal-religious tests of whether the lex Clodia is to be regarded as lawfully passed.
